quality.rationale: 需跨度规间断面进行爱因斯坦张量积分求表面应力，结合比霍夫定理与坐标匹配，逻辑严密且具天体物理背景

origin.question: [文件 1: 1_s16_16.14.md]Problem 16.14. Calculate the surface stresses in a static self-gravitating shell of mass M and circumference $2\pi R$ . What is the proper surface mass density? Compare the stresses to the Newtonian limit when $\mathbf{R} >> \mathbf{M}$ .## SolutionSolution 16.14.
origin.answer: $\Lambda_{\hat{0}}^{\hat{0}} = -\frac{M}{4\pi R^2(1+M/2R)^3}$, $\Lambda_{\hat{r}}^{\hat{r}} = 0$, $\Lambda_{\hat{\theta}}^{\hat{\theta}} = \Lambda_{\hat{\phi}}^{\hat{\phi}} = \frac{M}{8\pi R^2}\left(\frac{M/2R}{1-M/2R}\right)\left(1+\frac{M}{2R}\right)^{-3}$; 固有面密度 $\sigma = \frac{M}{4\pi R^2(1+M/2R)^4}$; 牛顿极限下 $\Lambda_{\hat{0}}^{\hat{0}} \to -\frac{M}{4\pi R^2}$, $\Lambda_{\hat{r}}^{\hat{r}} = 0$, $\Lambda_{\hat{\theta}}^{\hat{\theta}} = \Lambda_{\hat{\phi}}^{\hat{\phi}} \to \frac{M^2}{16\pi R^3}$
origin.solution: The metric cannot be continuous across the shell in curvature coordinates, because $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{rr}} = (1 - 2\mathfrak{m}(\mathbf{r}) / \mathbf{r})^{-1}$ and $\mathfrak{m}(\mathbf{r})$ changes across the shell. Therefore we use isotropic coordinates, in which the metric will be continuous and (matching flat space inside to mass M outside) has the form$$\mathrm {d s}^{2} = - \mathrm {e}^{\gamma} \mathrm {d t}^{2} + \mathrm {e}^{\alpha} \left(\mathrm {d r}^{2} + \mathrm {r}^{2} \mathrm {d}\Omega^{2}\right) $$$$e^{\gamma} = \left\{\begin{array}{l l} \left(1 - \frac {M}{2 r}\right)^{2} \left(1 + \frac {M}{2 r}\right)^{- 2} & r > R \\ \left(1 - \frac {M}{2 R}\right)^{2} \left(1 + \frac {M}{2 R}\right)^{- 2} & r < R \end{array}\right.$$$$e^{\alpha} = \left\{\begin{array}{l l} \left(1 + \frac {M}{2 r}\right)^{4} & r > R \\ \left(1 + \frac {M}{2 R}\right)^{4} & r < R. \end{array}\right.$$In Equations (1) and (2) we have used the fact that the geometry is Schwarzschild outside the shell and flat in the interior (Birkhoff's Theorem - see Problem 16.3). Also, the surface of the shell is located at $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{R}$ (in isotropic radial coordinate distance).Next we define the integrated stress, $\Lambda_{\hat{\beta}}^{\hat{a}}$ in the following invariant manner$$\Lambda^{\hat {a}}_{\hat {\beta}} \equiv \underset {\epsilon\rightarrow 0} {\operatorname {L I M}} \int_{R - \epsilon}^{R + \epsilon} T^{\hat {a}}_{\hat {\beta}} d \hat {r} = \underset {\epsilon\rightarrow 0} {\operatorname {L I M}} \int_{R - \epsilon}^{R + \epsilon} T^{\hat {a}}_{\hat {\beta}} e^{\frac {1}{2} a} d r.$$ The components of $\Lambda$ may be evaluated by using the Einstein field equations and by noting that, according to Equations (2) and (3), only terms in $\mathbf{G}_{\nu}^{\mu}$ with second derivatives of the metric potentials can contribute.The components of $\mathbf{G}_{\nu}^{\mu}$ for the metric of Equation (1) are (see Problem 9.20)$$G_{0}^{0} \sim a^{\prime \prime} e^{-a} \sim - \frac {d}{d r^{2}} (e^{-a})$$$$G_{r}^{r} \sim 0$$$$G_{\theta}^{\theta} = G_{\phi}^{\phi} \sim \frac {1}{2}. e^{-a}(a^{\prime \prime} + \gamma^{\prime \prime}). \sim - \frac {1}{2}. \left[ \frac {d^{2}}{dr^{2}}(e^{-a}) - \frac {d}{dr}(e^{-a}\gamma^{\prime})\right]$$where $\sim$ denotes the process of discarding all terms not involving second derivatives. Using Equations (3) and (4), and the Einstein field equations, we get$$\Lambda^{\widehat{0}}_{0} = \Lambda^{0}_{0} = -(8\pi)^{-1}\lim_{\epsilon \to 0}\int_{R - \epsilon}^{R + \epsilon}e^{\alpha /2}\frac{d^{2}}{dr^{2}} (e^{-\alpha})dr\sim -(8\pi)^{-1}\lim_{\epsilon \to 0}e^{\alpha /2}\frac{d}{dr}(e^{-\alpha})\bigg|_{R - \epsilon}^{R + \epsilon}$$or$$\Lambda_{\widehat {0}}^{\widehat {0}} = \frac {- M}{4 \pi R^{2}(1 + M/2R)^{3}}$$$$\Lambda_{r}^{r} = \Lambda_{r}^{r} = 0$$$$\Lambda^{\widehat{\theta}}_{\widehat{\theta}} = \Lambda^{\widehat{\phi}}_{\widehat{\phi}} = -(8\pi)^{-1} \lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \int_{R-\epsilon}^{R+\epsilon} \frac{1}{2} e^{\alpha/2} \left[ \frac{d^2}{dr^2}(e^{-\alpha}) - \frac{d}{dr}(e^{-\alpha}\gamma')\right] dr = -(8\pi)^{-1} \lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \frac{1}{2} e^{\alpha/2} \left[ \frac{d}{dr}(e^{-\alpha}) - e^{-\alpha}\gamma'\right]\Big|_{R-\epsilon}^{R+\epsilon}$$or$$\Lambda^{\widehat{\theta}}_{\widehat{\theta}} = \frac{M}{8\pi R^{2}} \left(\frac{M/2R}{1 - M/2R}\right) \left(1 + \frac{M}{2R}\right)^{-3}.$$ To have proper interpretation of Equations (4) to (6) we must remember that $R$ denotes the radius of the shell in isotropic coordinates. In curvature coordinates, the radius $\mathcal{R}$ is$$\mathcal{R} = R(1 + M/2R)^{2}.$$ The proper surface mass density is just$$\frac{M}{4\pi \mathcal{R}^{2}} = \frac{M}{4\pi R^{2}(1 + M/2R)^{4}}.$$ The Newtonian limit of Equations (4) to (6) is just$$\Lambda_{\hat{0}}^{\hat{0}} \to - \frac{M}{4\pi R^{2}}$$$$\Lambda_{\hat{r}}^{\hat{r}} = 0$$$$\Lambda_{\hat{\theta}}^{\hat{\theta}} = \Lambda_{\hat{\phi}}^{\hat{\phi}} \to \frac{M^{2}}{16\pi R^{3}}.$$
